skill.name: connecting-to-logseq
skill.description: Helps configure and troubleshoot connections between AI assistants and Logseq graphs through the local HTTP API, Logseq CLI, or MCP server.
skill.summary: Connect AI assistants to local Logseq graphs through HTTP API, CLI, or MCP workflows.
skill.author: C0ntr0lledCha0s

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found many command, network, environment, and weak-crypto patterns, but most high-volume hits are Markdown examples rather than executable skill code. The confirmed risks are legitimate for this integration: Bash-enabled setup guidance, local Logseq API calls with bearer tokens, environment variable handling, and local graph filesystem access. No prompt injection attempt or confirmed malicious data exfiltration was found.

medium_findings.title: Local Command Execution Is Required for Setup and Troubleshooting
medium_findings.description: The skill declares Bash access and guides users to run Python, npm, npx, curl, and Logseq CLI commands. This appears necessary for Logseq setup, but commands can change local software, install packages, or access graph data if followed without review.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The evidence directly shows Bash permission and documented commands. The context is legitimate setup documentation, so the risk is operational rather than malicious.

medium_findings.title: Authenticated Local Logseq API Can Read and Modify Graph Data
medium_findings.description: The skill documents authenticated POST requests to the Logseq localhost API and includes methods for reading pages, querying the database, and inserting blocks. This is expected behavior, but token misuse could expose or modify private notes.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The locations show the endpoint, bearer token use, and read/write Logseq methods. The API is localhost, which lowers exposure compared with an external endpoint.

medium_findings.title: Local Graph Filesystem Access May Expose Sensitive Notes
medium_findings.description: CLI guidance operates on graph paths, exports graph data, and references SQLite-backed graph storage. This is core functionality, but users should understand that graph exports and direct graph reads may include private notes.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file locations clearly show graph path and export behavior. There is no evidence of covert exfiltration, but graph contents are sensitive by nature.

low_findings.title: Localhost URLs Are Expected for This Integration
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URL and IP findings point to 127.0.0.1 Logseq API examples. This is not an external exfiltration endpoint, but users should avoid exposing the Logseq API port to a network.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: All cited network addresses are localhost examples, and the guide explicitly says not to expose the port. This makes the static network finding mostly expected behavior.

low_findings.title: Markdown Fences Caused Many Static Command False Positives
low_findings.description: Many Ruby backtick and weak-cryptography alerts are caused by Markdown inline code, fenced command examples, and ordinary words. No Ruby shell backtick execution or weak cryptographic algorithm implementation was found in the reviewed files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited locations are documentation code fences or inline code examples, not executable source files. The reviewed content does not define Ruby execution paths or cryptographic routines.

low_findings.title: Troubleshooting Suggests Displaying Token State
low_findings.description: The troubleshooting guide suggests echoing token values or token status during diagnostics. This can help debugging, but users should avoid pasting terminal output that contains real tokens into shared channels.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The locations show token-related diagnostic commands. The risk depends on user behavior and does not show automated leakage.

dangerous_patterns.title: Bash-Enabled Skill With Setup Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill is allowed to use Bash and includes setup commands for Python scripts, npm package installation, and server builds. Commands should be reviewed before execution, especially global package installs.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command capability and examples are explicit. They are not inherently malicious, but they are a meaningful execution surface.

dangerous_patterns.title: Bearer Token Sent to Local HTTP API
dangerous_patterns.description: Examples send LOGSEQ_API_TOKEN in Authorization headers to the local Logseq API. This is normal for Logseq automation, but the token authorizes access to the user graph.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The cited examples directly show bearer token use. The destination is localhost, so the pattern is sensitive but not evidence of malicious exfiltration.

content.user_title: Connect Claude and Codex to Logseq
content.value_statement: Connecting AI tools to a local Logseq graph can be difficult because API, CLI, and MCP paths differ. This skill gives focused setup and troubleshooting guidance for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code workflows.
actual_capabilities: Explains how to enable the Logseq HTTP API and create an authorization token.
Documents localhost API request formats for reading pages, blocks, and graph data.
Provides Logseq CLI setup, query, export, and troubleshooting guidance.
Shows MCP server build steps and how it depends on the Logseq HTTP API.
Includes an example environment configuration for API, CLI, and MCP backends.
limitations: Requires the user to install and run Logseq for HTTP API and MCP workflows.
Does not include the referenced setup scripts or MCP server implementation in this skill folder.
Cannot protect tokens if users paste terminal output into shared conversations.
CLI behavior depends on the installed @logseq/cli package and local graph format.

use_cases.title: Set Up Local Logseq Access
use_cases.description: Configure the HTTP API endpoint, token storage, and backend choice for an AI assistant.
use_cases.target_user: Knowledge workers using Logseq with Claude or Codex

use_cases.title: Debug Connection Failures
use_cases.description: Check ports, tokens, graph paths, and Logseq settings when API or CLI calls fail.

use_cases.title: Prepare Automation Workflows
use_cases.description: Use CLI and API examples to query, export, or update graph information in controlled workflows.
use_cases.target_user: Teams building note and knowledge graph automation

prompt_templates.title: Beginner Setup Check
prompt_templates.scenario: First connection to a local Logseq graph
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me connect Claude to my local Logseq graph. Walk me through the HTTP API setup and token configuration.

prompt_templates.title: Fix API Authentication
prompt_templates.prompt: My Logseq HTTP API returns unauthorized errors. Help me check token setup without exposing the token value.

prompt_templates.title: Choose a Backend
prompt_templates.scenario: Compare HTTP API, CLI, and MCP options
prompt_templates.prompt: Compare the Logseq HTTP API, CLI, and MCP server options for my workflow. Recommend one for local graph queries and occasional writes.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a Safe Automation
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a safe Logseq automation plan that reads task pages, exports selected data, and avoids leaking API tokens or private notes.

output_examples.input: Connect my assistant to Logseq for the first time.
output_examples.output: A setup checklist covering Developer mode, HTTP API enablement, token creation, local endpoint configuration, and a safe test request.
A troubleshooting sequence that checks whether Logseq is running, confirms the port, reviews firewall settings, and validates the API endpoint.

output_examples.input: Should I use CLI or HTTP API for automation?
output_examples.output: A backend recommendation based on whether Logseq is running, whether writes are needed, and whether the workflow runs locally or in CI.

best_practices: Store Logseq tokens in environment variables and avoid printing real token values.
Review every shell command before running it, especially global package installs.
Keep the Logseq HTTP API bound to localhost and do not expose the port externally.
anti_patterns: Do not commit env files that contain real Logseq API tokens.
Do not paste diagnostic output with tokens or private graph paths into public chats.
Do not run write operations against a graph without backups or user confirmation.

faq.question: Does this skill send data to an external service?
faq.answer: No external endpoint was found. The documented API examples target the local Logseq server at 127.0.0.1.

faq.question: Does Logseq need to be running?
faq.answer: Yes for HTTP API and MCP workflows. CLI workflows can work offline against local graph files.

faq.question: Can this skill write to my Logseq graph?
faq.answer: Yes. The documented API methods include insert and update operations, so review write prompts carefully.

faq.question: How should I store the API token?
faq.answer: Use an environment variable such as LOGSEQ_API_TOKEN. Avoid storing real tokens in committed configuration files.

faq.question: Why is the security risk medium?
faq.answer: The skill uses local commands, filesystem paths, and bearer tokens. These are legitimate but sensitive capabilities.

faq.question: Does this include a working MCP server?
faq.answer: This skill references MCP server setup, but the reviewed folder only includes guidance and configuration examples.
